title: Migrate all plaintext credentials from ConfigMap to Kubernetes Secrets and enforce secretKeyRef usage
description: Refactor infrastructure configuration to ensure no credentials (database, Redis, S3, Signal-CLI, external APIs) are stored in plaintext within ConfigMaps. Update all affected deployments and manifests to reference credentials exclusively via Kubernetes Secrets using secretKeyRef. Enable etcd encryption at rest for Secrets. Audit all envFrom usages to confirm no secrets are leaked into ConfigMap values.
subtasks: Identify all credentials currently embedded in the sigma1-infra-endpoints ConfigMap (POSTGRES_URL, REDIS_URL, etc.)
Create or update Kubernetes Secrets for all credentials (database, Redis, S3, Signal-CLI, external APIs)
Update all Deployment/StatefulSet manifests to use secretKeyRef for credentials instead of envFrom ConfigMap
Remove all password-bearing values from the ConfigMap, leaving only host/port/bucket references
Enable etcd encryption at rest for Kubernetes Secrets (if not already enabled)
Audit all envFrom usages in manifests to ensure no secrets are referenced in ConfigMap values
Test all service startups to ensure they can access credentials via Secrets
Run a security scan to confirm no credentials are present in any ConfigMap
Document the new credential management approach
acceptance_criteria: No credentials are present in any ConfigMap (verified by searching all ConfigMaps for password/API key patterns)
All deployments use secretKeyRef for credentials
etcd encryption at rest is enabled for Secrets (verified via Kubernetes API server config or managed service settings)
All services start and function correctly with credentials sourced from Secrets
Security scan (e.g., kube-hunter, kubesec) confirms no secret leakage in ConfigMaps

title: Implement shared authentication and authorization middleware for all backend APIs
description: Design and implement a shared authentication and authorization middleware for all backend API services. This includes JWT validation, token issuance, service-to-service authentication, and application-level RBAC. Define roles and permissions for all endpoints and tools, and integrate authorization checks into the Morgan agent MCP tools. Document the authentication architecture and permission matrix.
subtasks: Design the authentication architecture: decide on JWT vs session-based, define token issuer, claims, and validation process
Implement a shared authentication library/middleware for JWT validation and token issuance
Integrate authentication middleware into all backend API services (Rust/Axum, Go/gRPC, Node.js/Elysia, etc.)
Define application-level RBAC roles (admin, operator, customer, service-account) and permissions per endpoint
Implement authorization middleware that checks JWT claims/roles against required permissions for each route
Integrate tool-level authorization in the Morgan agent MCP tools, validating user roles before invoking privileged actions
Document the permission matrix and update API documentation to reflect required roles/permissions
Test all endpoints and MCP tools for correct authentication and authorization enforcement
Run OWASP ZAP or similar scan to verify endpoints are protected
acceptance_criteria: All backend APIs enforce authentication and reject unauthenticated requests
RBAC is enforced for all endpoints and MCP tools (admin-only endpoints/tools are inaccessible to non-admin users)
Morgan agent tools validate user roles before performing privileged actions
Permission matrix is documented and available to developers
Security scan confirms no unauthenticated or unauthorized access to protected endpoints

title: Harden inbound webhook endpoints with signature verification and source validation
description: Implement request signature verification for all inbound webhook endpoints (Twilio, Signal-CLI, social media callbacks). Use Twilio request validation, restrict Signal-CLI callbacks to localhost, and add source IP restrictions or shared secrets for other webhooks. Ensure all webhook handlers reject unsigned or spoofed requests.
subtasks: Implement Twilio request signature verification using X-Twilio-Signature header for all Twilio webhook endpoints
Restrict Signal-CLI callback endpoints to accept connections only from localhost (sidecar container)
For social media API callbacks, implement signature or shared secret validation if supported
Add source IP restrictions for webhook endpoints where signature validation is not available
Update documentation for all webhook endpoints to specify required validation
Test all webhook endpoints with valid and invalid signatures/IPs
Run a security scan (e.g., OWASP ZAP) against webhook endpoints
acceptance_criteria: All inbound webhook endpoints reject requests with invalid or missing signatures
Signal-CLI callbacks are only accepted from localhost
Social media callbacks enforce signature/shared secret validation where possible
Security scan confirms no spoofing or injection vulnerabilities on webhook endpoints

title: Enforce container image pinning and implement supply chain scanning for all images
description: Pin all container images (including Signal-CLI and all custom/third-party images) to specific digests or semantic version tags. Integrate container image vulnerability scanning (Trivy, Grype, or Snyk) into the CI/CD pipeline and block deployment of images with critical/high CVEs. Enforce minimal base image usage across all services.
subtasks: Update all Kubernetes manifests to use pinned image tags or digests for every container (no :latest tags)
Scan all images (custom and third-party) with Trivy, Grype, or Snyk before deployment
Block deployment of images with critical or high CVEs
Document and enforce a policy for image pinning and scanning in the CI/CD pipeline
Review and update base images to use minimal (distroless, scratch, alpine) where possible
Test deployment of all services with pinned images
Run a supply chain security scan and document results
acceptance_criteria: No container images use :latest or unpinned tags
CI/CD pipeline blocks images with critical/high CVEs
All images are scanned before deployment and scan reports are archived
All base images are minimal unless justified
Security scan confirms no unpinned or vulnerable images are deployed

title: Implement dependency auditing and version pinning for all backend services
description: Set up recurring CI tasks to audit dependencies for all backend services using cargo audit, npm audit/snyk, and govulncheck. Ensure all dependencies are pinned via lock files and configure automated update PRs with Dependabot or Renovate.
subtasks: Configure CI jobs to run cargo audit for Rust services, npm audit/snyk for Node.js services, and govulncheck for Go services
Verify all dependency lock files (Cargo.lock, package-lock.json, go.sum) are committed and up to date
Set up Dependabot or Renovate to automatically open PRs for dependency updates
Block merges for PRs that introduce critical/high vulnerabilities
Document the dependency management and auditing process
Test CI pipeline by introducing a known vulnerable dependency and verifying the audit fails
acceptance_criteria: CI pipeline fails on critical/high dependency vulnerabilities
All services have up-to-date lock files committed
Dependabot or Renovate is active and opening update PRs
Audit logs are available for all dependency scans
No known vulnerable dependencies are present in production

title: Classify and protect PII in customer vetting, CRM, and finance data flows
description: Classify data sensitivity levels for all PII handled by the Customer Vetting, CRM, and Finance services. Implement column-level encryption for highly sensitive fields, define data retention policies, and plan for data subject access/deletion requests (DSAR) in compliance with GDPR or similar regulations.
subtasks: Inventory all PII fields in the Customer Vetting, CRM, and Finance service data models
Classify data by sensitivity (e.g., credit scores, payment details as highly sensitive)
Implement column-level encryption for highly sensitive fields in PostgreSQL
Define and document data retention policies for all PII
Design and document a process for handling DSAR and right-to-deletion requests
Update API documentation to reflect PII handling and retention policies
Test encryption and retention policy enforcement
Run a privacy impact assessment
acceptance_criteria: All PII fields are classified and documented
Highly sensitive fields are encrypted at rest
Data retention policies are enforced and documented
DSAR/right-to-deletion process is documented and testable
Privacy impact assessment is completed and reviewed

title: Enforce TLS for all database and Redis connections
description: Configure PostgreSQL and Redis to require TLS connections. Update all connection strings to specify sslmode=require (or verify-full) and enable TLS on Redis. Ensure all in-cluster database traffic is encrypted.
subtasks: Enable TLS on PostgreSQL (CloudNative-PG) and Redis (Valkey operator)
Update all service connection strings to use sslmode=require or sslmode=verify-full for PostgreSQL
Enable and configure TLS for Redis connections
Update all application configurations to use TLS for database and cache connections
Test all services for successful TLS connections
Run a network capture to verify traffic is encrypted
acceptance_criteria: All database and Redis connections use TLS (verified by connection parameters and network capture)
Services fail to connect if TLS is disabled
No plaintext database credentials or traffic is observed in the cluster

title: Harden file upload endpoints with malware scanning and image re-encoding
description: Enhance file upload validation by implementing server-side image re-encoding to strip EXIF/metadata, malware/virus scanning for all uploads, and validation of image dimensions. Protect against zip bombs and decompression attacks.
subtasks: Integrate a malware/virus scanning solution (e.g., ClamAV or cloud scanning service) for all uploaded files
Re-encode uploaded images server-side to strip EXIF metadata and normalize format
Validate image dimensions in addition to file type and size
Implement checks to detect and reject zip bombs or decompression attacks
Update file upload documentation to reflect new validation steps
Test uploads with files containing EXIF data, malware signatures, and oversized/invalid images
acceptance_criteria: All uploaded images are re-encoded and have EXIF/metadata stripped
Malware-infected files are rejected
Oversized or invalid-dimension images are rejected
Security scan confirms no file upload vulnerabilities

title: Implement CSRF protection for all state-changing endpoints
description: Implement CSRF protection for all POST/PUT/DELETE endpoints that may be accessed via browser-based requests. If session-based authentication is used, require CSRF tokens. If JWT bearer tokens are used, ensure they are sent in the Authorization header and not cookies. Document the CSRF strategy.
subtasks: Determine authentication method in use (session-based or JWT)
If session-based, implement CSRF token generation and validation for all state-changing endpoints
If JWT-based, enforce use of Authorization header and reject requests with JWT in cookies
Update API documentation to specify CSRF protection requirements
Test all state-changing endpoints for CSRF vulnerability using automated tools (e.g., OWASP ZAP)
Document the chosen CSRF mitigation strategy
acceptance_criteria: All state-changing endpoints are protected against CSRF attacks
Automated security scan confirms no CSRF vulnerabilities
CSRF strategy is documented and reflected in API docs

title: Implement security event alerting for authentication and data access anomalies
description: Add security-specific alert rules to the monitoring stack. Alert on brute force attempts, unusual API key usage, privilege escalation attempts, and anomalous data access patterns. Integrate these alerts with the audit log pipeline.
subtasks: Define alert rules for failed authentication attempts, unexpected Secret access, API calls from unusual IPs, and bulk data access
Configure Prometheus, Alertmanager, or SIEM to trigger alerts based on these rules
Integrate alerting with the existing audit log pipeline
Test alerting by simulating brute force attempts and privilege escalation
Document all security alert rules and response procedures
acceptance_criteria: Alerts are triggered for >10 failed auth attempts from the same source in 5 minutes
Alerts fire on Secret access by unexpected service accounts
Alerts fire on API calls from unusual IPs or bulk data access
All alert rules are documented and tested

title: Harden public web chat endpoint with authentication, rate limiting, and abuse prevention
description: Implement anonymous session tokens with server-side validation for the public web chat endpoint. Add progressive rate limiting, CAPTCHA challenges after abuse signals, input sanitization, and prompt injection detection. Set connection and message size limits.
subtasks: Implement anonymous session tokens with server-side validation (not just localStorage)
Add progressive rate limiting that tightens with abuse signals (e.g., rapid messaging, repeated failed attempts)
Integrate CAPTCHA challenge after N messages from unauthenticated sessions
Sanitize all user input and implement prompt injection detection before passing messages to the AI agent
Set strict connection and message size limits on the WebSocket endpoint
Test the endpoint for abuse scenarios (spam, prompt injection, resource exhaustion)
Run OWASP ZAP or similar scan against the web chat endpoint
acceptance_criteria: Web chat endpoint enforces session tokens and server-side validation
Rate limiting and CAPTCHA are triggered on abuse patterns
Input sanitization and prompt injection detection are effective
Connection and message size limits are enforced
Security scan confirms no abuse or injection vulnerabilities
